title: Best Footrests for Under Desk 2026
excerpt: An ergonomic footrest improves posture, reduces back strain, and increases comfort at your desk. We found the best footrests for home and office use in 2026.
author: CompareElite Staff
intro: Poor leg and foot positioning at a desk is a primary contributor to lower back pain, hip strain, and poor posture. When your feet don't rest comfortably flat on the floor — because the desk is too high, your chair is set too tall to reach the keyboard, or your legs are too short — a footrest is the ergonomic solution. Footrests raise your feet to the proper height, keeping knees at a 90-degree angle and reducing the pressure on your lower back and thighs. We tested the best under-desk footrests to find the ones that genuinely improve sitting posture and comfort during long work sessions.

buying_guide.title: Height and Angle Adjustment
buying_guide.body: The most important feature in a footrest is adjustability. Fixed-height footrests only work for users at a specific chair/desk combination. Adjustable-height footrests (typically 3–5 preset heights or continuously adjustable) accommodate different users, chair heights, and desk heights. Angle adjustment matters too: a flat footrest works for some, but many users prefer a slight incline (10–30 degrees) that maintains a natural ankle angle. Some premium footrests offer independent control of height and angle, letting you dial in the perfect position. When selecting a footrest, start by measuring the gap between the floor and your feet when seated at your optimal keyboard/monitor height — that measurement determines the height range you need.

buying_guide.title: Platform Size and Foot Coverage
buying_guide.body: Footrests range from small kidney-shaped platforms (supporting only the heels/balls of feet) to large full-length platforms covering the entire foot from heel to toe. Larger platforms allow more foot position variety — you can shift your feet, change your stance, or place one foot up and one flat. For standing ergonomists who alternate sitting positions, an extended platform is worth the extra space. Narrower footrests are less obtrusive under the desk and can be tucked away when not needed. Width matters for dual monitors or shared desk setups — measure the available floor space under your desk before purchasing a wide footrest.

buying_guide.title: Surface Texture: Non-Slip and Massage Features
buying_guide.body: Footrest surfaces range from smooth plastic to textured rubber to massage nubs. Smooth surfaces allow shoes to slide — acceptable for users who keep shoes on. Non-slip rubber or textured surfaces grip socked feet and shoes — better for active foot positioning. Massage footrests with nodule surfaces claim to improve circulation by stimulating pressure points on the sole — a genuine benefit for users who experience foot fatigue or swelling during long sitting periods. Rocking or tilting footrests allow feet to move through their natural range of motion rather than staying static, which improves circulation and reduces the muscle fatigue of completely static positioning.

buying_guide.title: Material and Durability
buying_guide.body: Footrests are built from three main materials. Plastic shell footrests are the most common — lightweight, easy to clean, and inexpensive. Quality varies enormously: cheap thin plastic cracks or deforms under consistent pressure; quality ABS or polycarbonate is durable enough for years of daily use. Foam footrests (memory foam or EVA foam) provide cushioning for socked-foot use — comfortable but wear faster than hard surfaces. Metal footrests (steel or aluminum) are the most durable but heaviest and typically the most expensive. For professional office environments where the footrest will see years of daily use, invest in quality plastic or metal construction.

buying_guide.title: Rocker vs Fixed Footrests
buying_guide.body: Static footrests hold feet in a fixed position at a set height. While better than no footrest, they don't address the circulation problems of completely static positioning. Rocker footrests tilt forward and backward, allowing feet to move through a rocking motion during the workday — activating calf muscles, improving blood flow, and reducing fidgeting that leads to foot tapping. Active footrests go further: some tilt in multiple directions, some incorporate a balance board-style platform. For users who find static seating uncomfortable or have circulatory issues, an active rocker footrest is significantly more beneficial than a static one. For users with balance or stability concerns, a static non-slip platform is safer.

buying_guide.title: Anti-Slip Base and Stability
buying_guide.body: A footrest that slides around the floor defeats its purpose and creates a safety hazard. Look for footrests with substantial rubber non-slip bases that grip carpet and hard floors equally. On hardwood, tile, or laminate floors, footrests can slide more easily — prioritize wide rubber base coverage for these surfaces. For carpeted floors, a wider base prevents the footrest from sinking or tipping on soft carpet. Stability under dynamic use (rocking, active movement) requires either a wide stable base or a low center of gravity — check user reviews specifically for stability comments if you're buying a rocker model. Weight also contributes to stability: heavier footrests shift less but are harder to reposition.

verdict: The Kensington SoleMate Comfort footrest is our top pick for its multi-angle adjustment and proven ergonomic design trusted by occupational therapists. For an active rocking alternative that keeps feet moving, the Everlasting Comfort Office Foot Rest is an excellent mid-range choice. Budget buyers get solid basic ergonomic support from the HUANUO Adjustable Footrest.

products.name: Kensington SoleMate Comfort Footrest
pros: 4-position height adjustment (0 to 4 inches)
Adjustable tilt angle (0 to 20 degrees)
Large non-slip textured platform accommodates both feet
Certified by occupational therapists for ergonomic benefit
Durable construction — lasts years of daily use
cons: Discrete height adjustments — no continuous range
Heavier than plastic alternatives at 2.4 lbs
Fixed anti-tip feet not adjustable for uneven floors

Suitable for socked foot use — extremely comfortable
Non-slip bottom grip
Lightweight for easy repositioning
cons: Foam compresses over time with heavy use
Best for lighter users under 220 lbs

faq.q: Do I need a footrest if I can touch the floor with my feet?
faq.a: Only if your feet rest flat on the floor with your knees at 90 degrees and thighs roughly parallel to the floor. If you've raised your chair to achieve the correct keyboard/mouse height and your feet now dangle or barely touch the floor, a footrest fills the gap. Conversely, if lowering your chair to reach the floor properly would put the keyboard too low, a footrest lets you keep the chair at the ergonomically correct keyboard height. Footrests are particularly beneficial for shorter users (under 5'4") with standard-height desks.

faq.q: Can a footrest help with lower back pain?
faq.a: Yes — when properly used. Lower back pain from sitting often stems from hip angle: if your knees are higher than your hips (common when the chair is too low), it flattens the lumbar curve and strains the lower back. A footrest raises your feet, which allows you to sit with hips slightly higher than knees, maintaining the natural lumbar curve. Combined with a quality ergonomic chair with lumbar support, a footrest is part of a comprehensive sitting ergonomics setup. If you have diagnosed back conditions, consult an occupational therapist for a full ergonomic assessment.

faq.q: How high should my footrest be?
faq.a: Measure from the floor to just below your dangling feet when seated at your optimal keyboard/mouse height. That measurement is your ideal footrest height. For most office workers, this ranges from 2 to 5 inches. When positioned correctly, your feet should rest flat on the footrest with your knees at approximately 90 degrees and thighs roughly parallel to the floor. The angle should feel comfortable and natural — not as if you're perching on your toes or pressing your heels back.

faq.q: Are rocking footrests better than static ones?
faq.a: For circulation and comfort during extended sessions, yes. The rocking motion activates calf muscles that pump blood upward, reducing foot and ankle swelling common during long sitting periods. Active footrests also reduce the urge to tap feet or fidget, which can be distracting in shared office environments. For users with balance concerns or those who prefer a stable fixed surface, a quality static adjustable footrest is more appropriate. Static footrests also work better for users who need precise foot positioning for pedal-based work (audio production, video editing).

faq.q: Can I use a footrest with a standing desk?
faq.a: Yes — and it's beneficial. When your standing desk is at sitting height, a footrest improves leg ergonomics as described above. Some footrests are also useful at standing height: an anti-fatigue mat with a raised section at one end lets you shift one foot up at a time while standing, reducing lower back fatigue (this is sometimes called a standing footrest). Products like the Topo Anti-Fatigue Mat include this raised contour. For full standing use, look for anti-fatigue mats with varied topography rather than standard flat footrests, which are designed for seated use only.
